summary:
The Donghak (Tonghak) Peasant Revolution of 1894–95 was a major popular uprising in Korea that grew from a regional peasant revolt into a nationwide movement and ultimately triggered the intervention of both China and Japan that led directly to the [[event:2550|First Sino-Japanese War]]. It was one of the largest and most significant grassroots social movements in Korean history.

Donghak ('Eastern Learning') was a syncretic religious movement founded in the 1860s that combined elements of Confucianism, Buddhism, Shamanism, and Taoism with a message of social equality, resistance to Western influence, and Korean national identity. The movement attracted a massive following among Korean peasants suffering under corrupt local officials, heavy taxation, and economic distress.

The immediate trigger for armed uprising was the execution of the Donghak founder's son in 1894 and continued government repression of the movement. Peasant forces under Jeon Bong-jun captured the provincial capital of Jeonju in May 1894 and established a reform administration in the southwestern Jeolla province, implementing egalitarian social reforms and punishing corrupt officials. The Joseon government, unable to suppress the rebellion militarily, requested Chinese military assistance.

The arrival of Chinese troops triggered Japan, which had its own interests in Korea, to send forces as well under the terms of a prior convention. The two foreign armies' presence created the confrontation that became the First Sino-Japanese War. When Japan and China began fighting, the Donghak peasant forces rose again in October 1894 in a second uprising specifically targeting the Japanese presence in Korea. This second uprising was crushed by combined Japanese and government forces. The Donghak Revolution thus paradoxically produced the very foreign domination it had sought to resist, as the war resulted in Korea falling increasingly under Japanese influence.
